text.text: ().

### Images from San Jose CR Museo

These images are copyright for San Jose CR Museo and allowed to be used according to the license determined by them.

To visualize all images, use the left and right arrows on the slide below.

1 / 2
Map of the "world" © Trustees of the British Museum (). Shared under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).
Related content:
  • Atlas: Comparing Worlds - Comparison between the world understood by the Babylonians and our current world. ()
  • Atlas: The Ancient Babylonian World Map - Babylon: Study of the first known world map. ()
  • History: Cradles of civilization (Part I) - While humanity originated in Africa, the first civilizations emerged in various regions around the world, such as Mesopotamia, the Andes, and Mesoamerica. ()
  • Understanding Texts: Anachronism - although it is a word unknown to many, it is an action frequently practiced. ()
  • Understanding Texts: Equality Levels - Some concepts, like the definition of a planet, change over time. Sometimes, objects with slightly different sizes can be considered equal. How can we use numbers to quantify equality? ()
  • Myths and Believes: Did texts written in Mesopotamia describe the world we know today? - To properly answer this question, it is necessary to understand what was meant by 'world' in ancient times. Since the first known world map did not even mention nearby regions, it is not possible to claim that they had any idea of the true size of the world when they referred to their 'known world'. ()
  • Myths and Believes: Has there never been a flood? - There have been several; after all, for a flood to occur, it is enough for torrential rains to cause major disasters. ()
2 / 2
